title_en: The Death of the Prophet and His Continuing Legacy
content_id:
Catatan al-Qasthallani tentang sakit terakhir dan wafatnya Nabi menghimpun riwayat-riwayat sahih dari Bukhari, Muslim, dan koleksi-koleksi besar lainnya untuk merekonstruksi momen paling bersejarah ini dalam sejarah Islam. Pada tahun kesebelas Hijriyah, setelah kembali dari Haji Wada', Nabi mulai mengalami sakit kepala yang hebat dan demam. Beliau terus memimpin kaum Muslim dalam shalat ketika kekuatannya memungkinkan, dan ketika tidak mampu, beliau mengarahkan Abu Bakr untuk memimpinnya sebagai penggantinya, sebuah arahan yang dipahami secara luas sebagai pertanda keinginannya mengenai kepemimpinan setelahnya. Pada hari-hari terakhir, beliau keluar dari kamarnya di masjid dengan kepala terikat, duduk di samping Abu Bakr, mengonfirmasi kepemimpinannya dalam shalat, dan berbicara kepada jamaah. Beliau juga dengan terkenal bersabda, sebagaimana dicatat oleh beberapa perawi: 'Semoga Allah melaknat orang-orang yang menjadikan kubur para nabi mereka sebagai tempat ibadah,' memperingatkan agar tidak mengagungkan kuburnya sendiri.

Kesedihan para sahabat atas wafatnya Nabi dicatat dalam beberapa riwayat paling mengharukan dalam seluruh literatur hadis. Umar ibn al-Khattab, seorang lelaki yang terkenal dengan keteguhan dan ketenangannya, awalnya menolak percaya bahwa Nabi telah wafat dan mengancam mereka yang mengatakannya. Abu Bakar-lah yang masuk, memastikan kematian, keluar, dan menyampaikan pidato yang memulihkan ketertiban: 'Barangsiapa menyembah Muhammad, Muhammad telah meninggal. Barangsiapa menyembah Allah, Allah Mahahidup dan tidak mati.' Beliau kemudian membacakan ayat: 'Muhammad hanyalah seorang rasul, telah berlalu sebelumnya para rasul' (3:144). Al-Qasthallani mencatat bahwa Umar kemudian berkata bahwa ketika mendengar ayat tersebut, ia merasakan seolah kedua kakinya lemas di bawahnya, kenyataan itu akhirnya menghantam dengan kekuatan penuh.

Kehadiran Nabi yang berkelanjutan melalui Sunnahnya adalah penegasan teologis sentral dari bab-bab penutup al-Qasthallani. Kematian fisik Nabi tidak mengakhiri petunjuknya: Al-Quran dan Sunnah sahih yang ditinggalkannya merupakan warisan hidup yang cukup untuk membimbing umatnya hingga Hari Kiamat. Al-Qasthallani membahas doktrin syafaat kenabian (syafa'ah) secara panjang lebar, menyebutkan riwayat-riwayat sahih dalam Bukhari dan Muslim di mana Nabi menggambarkan perannya pada Hari Kiamat sebagai pemberi syafaat bagi umatnya. Ini termasuk di antara anugerah-anugerah istimewa (mawahib) kenabiannya: kepeduliannya kepada umatnya tidak berakhir dengan kematiannya melainkan berlanjut di alam ghaib.

Al-Qasthallani menutup dengan menegaskan pentingnya bershalawat kepada Nabi (al-shalatu alan-nabi), yang diperintahkan Al-Quran dan yang digambarkan Nabi sebagai sarana agar kaum beriman tetap terhubung dengannya. Beliau mencatat bahwa setiap shalawat yang disampaikan oleh seorang mukmin disampaikan kepada Nabi, yang membalasnya, sehingga terjalinlah hubungan hidup antara umat dan nabinya melampaui batas kematian. Al-Mawahib al-Ladunniyyah ditutup sebagaimana pembukaannya, dengan penegasan keunggulan unik dan komprehensif Nabi Muhammad sebagai anugerah rahmat ilahi bagi seluruh alam: 'Dan tidaklah Kami mengutusmu melainkan sebagai rahmat bagi seluruh alam' (21:107). Karya ini secara keseluruhan adalah persembahan pengabdian ilmiah, dipersembahkan sebagai rasa syukur atas rahmat tersebut.
content_en:
Al-Qastallani's account of the Prophet's final illness and death brings together authenticated narrations from al-Bukhari, Muslim, and other major collections to reconstruct this most consequential moment in Islamic history. In the eleventh year of the Hijra, following his return from the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet began to experience severe headaches and fever. He continued to lead the Muslims in prayer when his strength permitted, and when it did not, he directed Abu Bakr to lead in his place, a direction widely understood as indicating his wish regarding the succession. In the final days, he emerged from his apartment in the mosque with his head bound, sat beside Abu Bakr, confirmed his leadership of the prayer, and addressed the congregation. He also famously said, as recorded by multiple narrators: 'Curse Allah upon those who took the graves of their prophets as places of worship,' warning against the veneration of his own grave.

The grief of the Companions upon the Prophet's death is recorded in some of the most moving narrations in all of hadith literature. Umar ibn al-Khattab, a man of famous strength and composure, initially refused to believe that the Prophet had died and threatened those who said so. It was Abu Bakr who entered, confirmed the death, emerged, and delivered the speech that restored order: 'Whoever worshipped Muhammad, Muhammad has died. Whoever worships Allah, Allah is living and does not die.' He then recited the verse: 'Muhammad is no more than a messenger; messengers have passed before him' (3:144). Al-Qastallani notes that Umar later said that when he heard that verse, he felt as if his legs gave way beneath him, the reality finally landing with full force.

The continuing presence of the Prophet through his Sunnah is the central theological affirmation of al-Qastallani's concluding chapters. The Prophet's physical death did not terminate his guidance: the Quran and authenticated Sunnah that he left constitute a living inheritance sufficient for the community's guidance until the Last Day. Al-Qastallani discusses the doctrine of prophetic intercession (shafa'a) at length, noting the authenticated narrations in Bukhari and Muslim in which the Prophet describes his role on the Day of Judgment as the intercessor for his community. This is among the distinguished gifts (mawahib) of his prophethood: his concern for his people did not end with his death but continues in the realm of the unseen.

Al-Qastallani concludes by affirming the significance of sending salutations upon the Prophet (al-salatu alan-nabi), which the Quran commands and the Prophet described as a means by which the believers remain connected to him. He notes that every salutation sent by a believer is conveyed to the Prophet, who returns it, establishing a living relationship between the community and its Prophet across the barrier of death. Al-Mawahib al-Ladunniyyah closes as it opened, with an affirmation of the unique and comprehensive excellence of the Prophet Muhammad as a gift of divine mercy to all of creation: 'And We have not sent you except as a mercy to all the worlds' (21:107). The work as a whole is an act of scholarly devotion, offered in gratitude for that mercy.
